caption: 100+ year innovation and leadership timeline: autopilot (1914), ring laser gyro (1966), first APU (1950s), HTF7000 (2004), CAES+Civitanavi M&A (2024).
HONA at a glance: $17.4B 2025 net sales, +12% organic, $4.3B adjusted EBIT; ES 39% / E&PS 31% / CS 30% and OE 15% / aftermarket 44% / D&S 41%; ~$19B backlog, content on ~90% of the in-service fleet.
Three-segment balance: ES $6.8B (~90% of aircraft use HONA avionics/nav), E&PS $5.4B (~47K APUs in service), CS $5.2B (>75% of flights start with HONA engine-start).

15-year margin transformation: HON Aerospace (ex-HTSI) sales $9.7B->$17.5B and segment margin 17.5% (2010) -> 25.5% (2019, +800bps) -> 26.1% (2025 adjusted); phase 1 portfolio pruning, phase 2 decoupled RMU-led growth.

2023-2025 track record: +11% organic net-sales CAGR, backlog +14% CAGR, $1.8B FY25 R&D (~10% of sales, 60% customer-funded); company-funded R&D above peer average (peers: Curtiss-Wright, GE, HEICO, Howmet, Rolls-Royce, RTX, Safran, TransDigm, Woodward).

Attractive A&D end markets: Commercial OE mid-to-high single digits, Commercial Aftermarket mid-single, Defense & Space mid-to-high single-digit market growth 2025-2030E; air traffic +4%, intl defense spend +7%.
Nose-to-tail content map across Commercial Air Transport, Business Aviation and Defense & Space; HONA content on 250+ in-production platforms spanning all three product segments.
Growth architecture: >$90B commercial+defense wins (2022-2025), 40+ major RMUs in process, +HSD international-defense growth 2025-2030E.
"Develop once, deploy everywhere" R&D engine: common sensors/core products (precision nav, APUs, air/thermal) reused across verticals to spread NPI cost and lift ROI, backed by ~$1.8B FY25 R&D.
Capital-allocation framework: invest in organic growth, then competitive dividend, select bolt-on M&A, and opportunistic buybacks -- anchored to a strong investment-grade balance sheet.

Leadership team: CEO Jim Currier (19 yrs at HON), long-tenured segment presidents (Marinick 37, Buddecke 28), but recent external hires for CFO Josh Jepsen (<1 yr, ex-Deere) and supply chain (Worthen, <1 yr).

Board of directors: independent chair Craig Arnold (ex-Eaton CEO), Gen. David Goldfein (ex-USAF Chief of Staff), Dr. Will Roper (ex-USAF/Space Force acquisition), Bill Ayer (ex-Alaska Air) -- heavy A&D/defense-procurement expertise.

Electronic Solutions at a glance: $6.8B sales, 5% organic, $2.0B adjusted EBIT; ~90% of aircraft use HONA avionics/nav, ~62K nav-and-sensors installed base, ~80% of EDS (ex-CAES) positions sole-sourced.

M&A rationale: CAES adds RF/electronic-warfare and missile-franchise presence (EW industry +6% CAGR); Civitanavi adds gyroscope tech and EU sovereign manufacturing (high-end inertial +11% CAGR) -- both framed as outgrowing their markets.
Anthem next-gen integrated cockpit: five platform wins and >$10B lifetime value; modular, cloud-connected, AI-capable; Bombardier R&D agreement and NXP compute partnership.

Engines & Power Systems at a glance: $5.4B sales, ~$1.1B adjusted EBIT; ~20K engines and ~47K APUs in service across ~120 aircraft types; organic 12% ex-2024 Bombardier and ex-Flexjet (21% incl. Bombardier).
Installed base drives aftermarket: HTF7000 business-jet engine base ~5% CAGR and commercial APU base ~9% CAGR to 2030E; 30+ year platform lives feed recurring high-margin service revenue.

Control Systems at a glance: $5.2B sales, 10% organic, $1.5B adjusted EBIT; >75% of commercial flights begin with HONA engine-start, >10K landings/day on HONA brakes, 10M passengers/day fly on CS content.
Power & Thermal Management on the F-35: 610 aircraft OE base, >1M flight hours, ~10% installed-base CAGR to 2030E, plus a large RMU retrofit opportunity across in-service F-35s.

Integrated operating model (HONA OS): supply-chain integration stated to support DOUBLING sales over the next 10 years (off FY24 revenue, ~7% CAGR) -- an aspiration above the formal 6-8% 2030 organic target.
Inventory turns declined from ~3-4x (2018) post-COVID; returning to the 2018 level is worth ~$1B of cash -- the working-capital lever behind FCF-faster-than-EBIT.

Bombardier ~$17B landmark agreement (Dec 2024): multi-system content across Anthem avionics, engines and next-gen SATCOM plus long-term services/upgrades -- source of the +$0.4B one-time 2024 E&PS benefit.
Global Commercial Aftermarket at a glance: $7.7B sales, 12% organic, 65K in-service fleet, 26 R&O facilities; RMUs ~10% of HONA sales ($1.6B) growing ~18% CAGR since 2021.
Two aftermarket models over a 50+ year life: electronics compound value through continuous software/RMU upgrades; mechanical products deliver recurring parts-and-services maintenance revenue.
Defense & Space at a glance: $7.1B (41% of 2025 revenue), 9% organic, ~30% international, content on 11 of 12 MAC priority missile programs across 300+ platforms.

Critical U.S. DoW missile supplier: long-term framework agreement drives 2-4x capacity expansion; content across long-range/interceptor/precision-guided programs (AMRAAM, Standard Missile, THAAD, PAC-3).

The annuity, quantified: commercial aftermarket:OE ratio 3:1, narrowbody APU aftermarket net sales >20x the initial OE sale, RMU net sales $1.6B (2025) growing double-digit -- the installed base, not new-build, is the profit engine.
2030 financial targets: 6-8% organic sales CAGR, adjusted EBIT >$6.5B, free cash flow >$4.0B (standalone non-GAAP) -- the first standalone forward guardrails.

Adjusted-EBIT bridge $4.3B (2025) -> $6.5B (2030E), ~9% CAGR: volume leverage + price-cost catch-up + productivity + growth investments, against a modest mix headwind; EBIT set to grow faster than 6-8% sales.
FCF bridge: 2025 $3.3B -> 2025 pro forma standalone $2.5B (after interest/trademark/standalone costs) -> >$4.0B by 2030E (~10% CAGR), ~100% long-term conversion.

Balance sheet: ~$16B debt / $1B cash (Mar 2026), ratings A3 / BBB+ / A-, maintenance capex only ~1-2% of sales; 2.5x medium-term target leverage is BELOW current ~3.7x gross, so near-term FCF is partly deleveraging.
